articles.headline: Your Deep Sleep Is a Hormone Factory. Here's What Happens When You Skip It.
articles.body: UC Berkeley scientists just mapped the exact brain circuit that controls growth hormone release during sleep. Published in Cell, the study shows deep, early-night sleep is when your body gets its biggest repair signal. That hormone builds muscle, burns fat, and keeps your brain sharp. Miss that window and output drops fast. In adults, low growth hormone is linked to weight gain, slower recovery, and higher risk of metabolic disease. The biggest pulse happens in the first two to three hours after you fall asleep. Alcohol before bed, a warm room, or a drifting bedtime all disrupt the circuit.
articles.takeaway: Lock in a consistent bedtime and keep your room cool so your body can actually do its overnight repair work.

product.name: Manta Sleep Mask
product.blurb: Total darkness is one of the highest-leverage sleep improvements you can make.

articles.headline: Creatine Isn't Just for Your Muscles. New Research Says It Protects Your Brain Too.
articles.body: A new study published in Nutrients found that a single dose of creatine improved cognitive performance by 12% during 21 hours of sleep deprivation. Researchers in Germany tested logical reasoning, language processing, and mental vigilance. Creatine won across the board. The thinking is that sleep-deprived brain cells are under stress, and that stress actually helps creatine get absorbed faster into the brain. For anyone running on four hours of sleep while managing work and kids, this is worth knowing. Creatine is already safe, cheap, and well-studied for physical performance.
articles.takeaway: If you're not already taking creatine daily, this is one more very good reason to start.

product.name: Thorne Creatine Monohydrate
product.blurb: The most researched supplement in history. Works for strength, brain health, and endurance.

articles.headline: Your Brain Is Running Your Endurance. Science Just Proved It.
articles.body: A new UT Southwestern study published in Neuron found that neurons in the brain, not just the heart or muscles, are what drive endurance improvements from exercise. Researchers identified a specific group of cells in the hypothalamus that become more active as training continues, forming a kind of memory of past workouts. When those neurons were blocked in mice, endurance gains stopped completely. When they were activated, gains kept coming even past the normal plateau. Most people credit their lungs and legs for fitness. Turns out the brain is running the show.
articles.takeaway: Consistency matters more than intensity. Every session is training your brain to expect more, so showing up regularly is the actual work.

product.name: Garmin Forerunner 255
product.blurb: The watch Chris trains with daily. Tracks every discipline without breaking the bank.

articles.headline: Exercise Doesn't Just Make You Fit. It Makes You Biologically Younger.
articles.body: A review published April 29 in The Lancet Healthy Longevity found that higher physical activity is significantly linked to lower biological age, measured by epigenetic clocks that track how fast your cells are actually aging. Your calendar age and your biological age are two different numbers. Exercise appears to move the second one backward. The effect showed up consistently across multiple aging markers. Researchers say the data is strong enough that physical activity may actually change how fast you age at the cellular level, not just how you feel.
articles.takeaway: You don't need a longevity drug. Regular movement is still the most evidence-backed tool for slowing biological aging.
